exercises.title: Identify Spring AI components
exercises.instructions:
Match each Spring AI component to its role:
A: ChatClient
B: SystemMessage
C: .call().content()
D: .stream().content()
E: temperature=0.1
F: PromptTemplate with .param()
exercises.expected_solution:
A: ChatClient
   The main API entry point for talking to an AI model
   Autoconfigured by Spring AI starter: inject ChatClient.Builder, call .build()
   Equivalent to: RestTemplate for HTTP, but for AI conversations

B: SystemMessage
   Defines the AI's role, identity, and constraints for the whole conversation
   Applied to every message: 'You are an HDFC Bank assistant...'
   Set once via .defaultSystem() on the builder

C: .call().content()
   Synchronous: waits for the full AI response, returns String
   Use for: background processing, batch jobs, API responses
   Blocks the calling thread until AI responds (typically 1-5 seconds)

D: .stream().content()
   Streaming: returns Flux<String> with tokens as they're generated
   Use for: user-facing chat UI (perceived latency much lower)
   Spring Boot + SSE: stream tokens to browser as they arrive

E: temperature=0.1
   Low temperature: deterministic, factual, consistent responses
   Range: 0.0 (always same answer) to 1.0+ (creative, varied)
   Banking: use 0.1-0.3 (facts, not poetry)
   Marketing copy: use 0.7-0.9 (varied, creative)

F: PromptTemplate with .param()
   Safe parameterized prompts (prevents prompt injection)
   .user(u -> u.text('Account: {id}, Q: {q}').param('id',id).param('q',q))
   Never: 'Answer: ' + userInput (injection risk!)

test_cases.expected:
A: ChatClient
   The main API entry point for talking to an AI model
   Autoconfigured by Spring AI starter: inject ChatClient.Builder, call .build()
   Equivalent to: RestTemplate for HTTP, but for AI conversations

B: SystemMessage
   Defines the AI's role, identity, and constraints for the whole conversation
   Applied to every message: 'You are an HDFC Bank assistant...'
   Set once via .defaultSystem() on the builder

C: .call().content()
   Synchronous: waits for the full AI response, returns String
   Use for: background processing, batch jobs, API responses
   Blocks the calling thread until AI responds (typically 1-5 seconds)

D: .stream().content()
   Streaming: returns Flux<String> with tokens as they're generated
   Use for: user-facing chat UI (perceived latency much lower)
   Spring Boot + SSE: stream tokens to browser as they arrive

E: temperature=0.1
   Low temperature: deterministic, factual, consistent responses
   Range: 0.0 (always same answer) to 1.0+ (creative, varied)
   Banking: use 0.1-0.3 (facts, not poetry)
   Marketing copy: use 0.7-0.9 (varied, creative)

F: PromptTemplate with .param()
   Safe parameterized prompts (prevents prompt injection)
   .user(u -> u.text('Account: {id}, Q: {q}').param('id',id).param('q',q))
   Never: 'Answer: ' + userInput (injection risk!)

hints: ChatClient = AI's RestTemplate
SystemMessage = job description for the AI
stream() = lower perceived latency for users

exercises.title: Complete the Spring AI ChatClient setup
exercises.instructions: Fill in the blanks to build a banking ChatClient.

blanks.hint: creates the ChatClient from the builder
extracts the String text from the AI response

hints: ChatClient.Builder is Spring-managed (autoconfigured)
.build() creates the final ChatClient
.call().content() returns the String response

exercises.title: Simple bank AI service
exercises.instructions:
Write HdfcAiService:
1. Inject ChatClient.Builder
2. defaultSystem: 'You are a concise HDFC Bank assistant. Answer banking questions only.'
3. answer(question): call + return content
4. @SpringBootTest with mocked AI: verify response structure

Note: Use @MockBean or @TestConfiguration to mock ChatClient in tests.
hints: ChatClient.Builder: Spring AI autoconfigures this — just inject it
.defaultSystem(): applies to every conversation in this ChatClient
.call().content(): synchronous response as String

exercises.title: Parameterized prompt template
exercises.instructions:
Write PaymentAiService with safe prompt templates:
1. classifyDispute(merchantName, amount, reason): prompt with 3 params
2. Use .text(template).param() — NOT string concatenation
3. System prompt: classify-only mode
4. @SpringBootTest: verify prompt built correctly (no injection risk)
hints: .text(template).param(key, value): safe parameterized prompt
String concatenation with user input: prompt injection risk
FRAUD/MERCHANT_ERROR/CUSTOMER_ERROR: system prompt forces specific output format

exercises.title: Structured output with BeanOutputConverter
exercises.instructions:
Write an AI service that returns structured Java objects:
1. DisputeClassification record: category, confidence, explanation
2. Use BeanOutputConverter<DisputeClassification>
3. AI returns JSON → Spring AI converts to record
4. @SpringBootTest: document the converter pattern
hints: BeanOutputConverter.getFormat(): generates JSON schema instructions for the AI
converter.convert(response): parses AI's JSON string → Java record
record fields: AI follows the schema — field names matter

exercises.title: Streaming AI response with SSE
exercises.instructions:
Write a streaming endpoint for HDFC Bank chat:
1. ChatController: POST /api/ai/chat/stream
2. Returns Flux<String> with produces=text/event-stream
3. chatClient.prompt().user(q).stream().content()
4. @WebMvcTest: endpoint exists and returns SSE content type
hints: TEXT_EVENT_STREAM_VALUE: SSE content type for browser streaming
Flux<String>: reactive stream — tokens emitted as AI generates them
Perceived latency: 300ms to first word vs 3-5s for full response

exercises.title: Multi-turn conversation with memory
exercises.instructions:
Implement conversation memory for HDFC Bank chat:
1. ConversationStore: Map<sessionId, List<Message>> in memory
2. ChatService: adds previous messages to each new prompt
3. Customer can reference earlier messages ('fix the error above')
4. @SpringBootTest (2 tests): first message, follow-up uses history
hints: Conversation history: past messages sent with every new prompt
UserMessage + AssistantMessage: Spring AI message types
Session = List<Message>: grows with each turn

exercises.title: AI with function calling (tool use)
exercises.instructions:
Connect the AI to a real banking function:
1. @Tool AccountBalanceTool: getBalance(accountId) returns String
2. ChatClient with tool registered
3. When user asks 'what is my balance?', AI calls the tool
4. @SpringBootTest: tool registered, function invoked on query
hints: @Tool: marks a method as callable by the AI
defaultTools(toolBean): registers tools with the ChatClient
AI decides when to call: 'what is my balance?' → AI calls getBalance() automatically

exercises.title: Rate limiting AI calls (cost control)
exercises.instructions:
Add rate limiting to AI calls (AI tokens cost money!):
1. AiRateLimiter: max 10 AI calls per merchant per minute
2. Bucket4j: token bucket per merchantId
3. AiService: check rate limit before calling AI
4. @SpringBootTest (2 tests): within limit passes, exceeded limit throws
hints: AI token costs money: each call = $$. Rate limiting prevents runaway costs
Per-merchant bucket: different limits for free vs premium plans
checkAndConsume before AI call: rate limit check is synchronous

exercises.title: AI prompt engineering analysis
exercises.instructions:
Analyze these system prompts for the Razorpay dispute classifier. For each, predict the likely AI behavior and rate its quality (1-5):

Prompt A: (empty system message)
Prompt B: 'You are helpful.'
Prompt C: 'Classify payment disputes as FRAUD, MERCHANT_ERROR, or CUSTOMER_ERROR. Return only the label.'
Prompt D: 'You are Razorpay's AI. Classify disputes. Accepted labels: FRAUD|MERCHANT_ERROR|CUSTOMER_ERROR. Return JSON: {category: string, confidence: 0-100}. Never add explanation. If unclear: confidence < 50.'
exercises.expected_solution:
Prompt A: (empty)
  Behavior: AI uses default helpful assistant behavior. May write paragraphs.
  Output might be: 'This appears to be a case of potential fraud based on...'
  Quality: 1/5 — no constraints, inconsistent output, cannot parse programmatically

Prompt B: 'You are helpful.'
  Behavior: slightly better, still no output format specified
  Output might be: 'I think this is a FRAUD case, though I'm not 100% certain...'
  Quality: 2/5 — too vague, output not machine-parseable

Prompt C: 'Classify... Return only the label.'
  Behavior: AI returns single label (FRAUD, MERCHANT_ERROR, or CUSTOMER_ERROR)
  Output: 'FRAUD'
  Quality: 4/5 — consistent, parseable, but no confidence score
  Limitation: no way to know how certain the AI is (confidence 51% vs 99% = both 'FRAUD')

Prompt D: Detailed with JSON + confidence
  Behavior: returns JSON with category and confidence
  Output: '{"category": "FRAUD", "confidence": 87}'
  Quality: 5/5 — parseable JSON, confidence enables routing (low confidence → human review)
  Best practice elements:
    - Exact output format specified
    - Allowed values enumerated
    - Edge case handled (unclear → low confidence)
    - No room for 'helpfully' adding explanation

LESSON: Prompt quality directly determines output parsability.
For production AI: always specify exact output format, allowed values, and edge case behavior.

test_cases.expected:
Prompt A: (empty)
  Behavior: AI uses default helpful assistant behavior. May write paragraphs.
  Output might be: 'This appears to be a case of potential fraud based on...'
  Quality: 1/5 — no constraints, inconsistent output, cannot parse programmatically

Prompt B: 'You are helpful.'
  Behavior: slightly better, still no output format specified
  Output might be: 'I think this is a FRAUD case, though I'm not 100% certain...'
  Quality: 2/5 — too vague, output not machine-parseable

Prompt C: 'Classify... Return only the label.'
  Behavior: AI returns single label (FRAUD, MERCHANT_ERROR, or CUSTOMER_ERROR)
  Output: 'FRAUD'
  Quality: 4/5 — consistent, parseable, but no confidence score
  Limitation: no way to know how certain the AI is (confidence 51% vs 99% = both 'FRAUD')

Prompt D: Detailed with JSON + confidence
  Behavior: returns JSON with category and confidence
  Output: '{"category": "FRAUD", "confidence": 87}'
  Quality: 5/5 — parseable JSON, confidence enables routing (low confidence → human review)
  Best practice elements:
    - Exact output format specified
    - Allowed values enumerated
    - Edge case handled (unclear → low confidence)
    - No room for 'helpfully' adding explanation

LESSON: Prompt quality directly determines output parsability.
For production AI: always specify exact output format, allowed values, and edge case behavior.

hints: Output format in system prompt: determines parsability
Confidence score: enables human-in-the-loop routing
Empty prompt: AI adds unsolicited explanation → can't parse

exercises.title: Complete HDFC customer support AI service
exercises.instructions:
Build complete HDFC Bank AI support system:
1. HdfcAiConfig: ChatClient with detailed system prompt + tool registration
2. AccountTool: getBalance(accountId), getLastTransactions(accountId)
3. SupportService: answer(sessionId, accountId, question) — with history
4. RateLimiter: 20 calls/minute per customer
5. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): tool works, rate limit enforced, history accumulates
hints: defaultTools(tools): register all @Tool methods from the component
ConcurrentHashMap: thread-safe session/rate-limit storage
History: 2 messages per turn (UserMessage + AssistantMessage)

exercises.title: AI response audit logging
exercises.instructions:
Compliance requirement: every AI call must be audited for RBI:
1. AiAuditLog entity: sessionId, merchantId, prompt, response, model, timestamp
2. AiAuditService: log every AI call (async, don't slow down the AI call)
3. Wrap ChatClient calls in try-finally to guarantee audit even on error
4. @DataJpaTest: audit log entries saved correctly
hints: @Async audit: don't slow down the AI response (audit in background)
hadError: audit even on exceptions (try-finally wrapper in calling service)
RBI compliance: every AI-generated bank communication must be logged

exercises.title: AI-powered fraud detection service
exercises.instructions:
Build Razorpay AI fraud detection:
1. TransactionContext: merchant, amount, location, deviceId, hour
2. FraudDetectionAi: AI scores transaction risk (0-100) + reason
3. FraudScore record: score, riskLevel (LOW/MEDIUM/HIGH), recommendation
4. Structured output with BeanOutputConverter
5. @SpringBootTest (3 tests): low-risk, high-risk, structured output parsed
hints: BeanOutputConverter.getFormat(): inject as .param() so AI knows the schema
Risk factors: hour 2-5am + new device + large amount + foreign city
FraudScore.score 71-100 = HIGH: route to human review or block

exercises.title: AI prompt injection protection
exercises.instructions:
Write PromptInjectionGuard:
1. Detect common injection patterns in user input
2. sanitize(userInput): flag or clean dangerous patterns
3. Patterns: 'ignore previous', 'system:', 'you are now', 'forget everything'
4. @SpringBootTest (4 tests): clean input passes, each injection pattern detected
hints: Pattern.CASE_INSENSITIVE: 'IGNORE' and 'ignore' both caught
Parameterized templates (.param()) are safer than string concat but injection guard adds defense-in-depth
Max length 2000: prevent token bombs (huge inputs = huge costs)

exercises.title: Complete M26 T1 Spring AI synthesis
exercises.instructions:
Build complete M26 T1 synthesis:
1. BankAiService: ChatClient + system prompt + tool
2. PromptGuard: injection detection
3. AiRateLimiter: 10 calls/minute per user
4. AuditLogger: log every call
5. @SpringBootTest (4 tests): service works, injection blocked, rate limit, audit logged
6. All 4 pass
exercises.starter_code: // Complete M26 T1 Spring AI synthesis
hints: Facade pattern: single entry point orchestrating guard → rate-limit → AI → audit
Rate limit exhausted: tokens=0 after 10 calls
Audit.count increments on every successful call
